questions.question: Why is AsyncStorage a poor choice for storing sensitive data like auth tokens or personal information in a React Native app?
options: AsyncStorage is too slow for repeated token access during API calls.

AsyncStorage stores data as plain text in an unencrypted SQLite database file on the device filesystem. On Android, this file is world-readable if the device is rooted. On iOS, it's readable without Keychain protection if the device backup is accessed. Auth tokens in AsyncStorage can be extracted from a compromised or backed-up device. SecureStore uses iOS Keychain and Android Keystore — hardware-backed encryption that survives factory-reset attacks and is not accessible even to other apps.
AsyncStorage is deprecated and no longer maintained by the React Native community.
AsyncStorage has a 100KB size limit that makes token storage impractical.
questions.answer: AsyncStorage stores data as plain text in an unencrypted SQLite database file on the device filesystem. On Android, this file is world-readable if the device is rooted. On iOS, it's readable without Keychain protection if the device backup is accessed. Auth tokens in AsyncStorage can be extracted from a compromised or backed-up device. SecureStore uses iOS Keychain and Android Keystore — hardware-backed encryption that survives factory-reset attacks and is not accessible even to other apps.
questions.explanation: Security rule: AsyncStorage = user preferences (theme, language, last tab). SecureStore = credentials, tokens, PII. MMKV default = same risk profile as AsyncStorage. You can enable MMKV encryption for sensitive data, but SecureStore uses platform-native credential storage purpose-built for this use case.

questions.question: In offline-first architecture, you save messages locally with synced=0 before attempting server sync, then set synced=1 after success. Why is this order critical?
options: Setting synced=0 first is a UI convention only — the operation order doesn't affect correctness.

If you attempt server sync FIRST without saving locally and the network times out or fails, the message is lost — the user typed it and pressed send but it disappeared. Save-locally-first guarantees the message exists on the device regardless of network outcome. The sync operation is then a best-effort upgrade from 'stored locally' to 'confirmed by server'. Even if sync fails 10 times, the message persists until a retry succeeds.
synced=0 first prevents duplicate messages from appearing if the app crashes.
The SQLite write must happen before the network call for transaction isolation purposes.
questions.answer: If you attempt server sync FIRST without saving locally and the network times out or fails, the message is lost — the user typed it and pressed send but it disappeared. Save-locally-first guarantees the message exists on the device regardless of network outcome. The sync operation is then a best-effort upgrade from 'stored locally' to 'confirmed by server'. Even if sync fails 10 times, the message persists until a retry succeeds.
questions.explanation: iMessage uses exactly this pattern: when you send a message with no signal, the message appears immediately with a 'Sending...' indicator. It retries in the background. If delivery succeeds later, the indicator changes to 'Delivered'. The message is NEVER lost. This is the standard behavior users expect from quality messaging apps.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of MMKV over AsyncStorage for reading frequently accessed key-value data like user preferences?
options: MMKV automatically replicates data to the cloud while AsyncStorage is device-local only.

MMKV provides a synchronous API backed by a high-performance C++ memory-mapped storage engine, with no bridge overhead (JSI direct access). AsyncStorage requires async/await for every read because it crosses the JS bridge to native, incurring ~2-10ms overhead per call. For settings accessed on every render (theme, language, user preferences), MMKV synchronous reads are effectively free vs AsyncStorage's async overhead accumulating across many reads.
MMKV supports larger values than AsyncStorage which has a 1MB limit.
MMKV automatically compresses stored values, reducing storage footprint by 50%.
questions.answer: MMKV provides a synchronous API backed by a high-performance C++ memory-mapped storage engine, with no bridge overhead (JSI direct access). AsyncStorage requires async/await for every read because it crosses the JS bridge to native, incurring ~2-10ms overhead per call. For settings accessed on every render (theme, language, user preferences), MMKV synchronous reads are effectively free vs AsyncStorage's async overhead accumulating across many reads.
questions.explanation: WeChat's RN team benchmarked MMKV at ~10x faster reads than AsyncStorage in a cold-read scenario (first read after app start). The synchronous API is also more ergonomic — no async/await needed: const isDark = storage.getBoolean('darkMode') ?? false can be called anywhere, even in synchronous Zustand store initialization.
